ATTRS: Direct regulator oversight of third parties

UK.reg: PRA · FCA · Bank of England · HM Treasury
I: PRA SS2/21 — Outsourcing and Third Party Risk Management (Supervisory Statement)
SS2/21 (reissued as Appendix 3 to PRA PS7/26)
Mar 2021; updated 15 Nov 2024; reissued 18 Mar 2026
31 Mar 2022 (original)
PRA
UK banks, PRA-designated investment firms, Solvency II insurers (incl. Lloyd's), UK branches of overseas banks/insurers.

Material outsourcing where defect/failure could materially impair UK financial stability, the firm's ability to meet Threshold Conditions/Fundamental Rules, or regulatory reporting (§5). Ch.5 now covers 'material third-party' (MTP) identification.

Scope; service & data-processing locations; sub-outsourcing controls & notification; data security/confidentiality (Ch.7); access/audit/information rights for firm, PRA & BoE (Ch.8); BCP & 'demonstrably successful stressed exit plans' (Ch.10).
No statutory hour-clock under SS2/21 itself; notify PRA 'promptly' (PRA Rulebook Notifications 2.3). 24-hour determination clock arrives via PS7/26.
Internal register of all outsourcing arrangements + separate register of non-outsourcing third-party arrangements (§4.13); available to PRA on request.
Indirect (via the regulated firm). Direct oversight sits in the separate CTP regime.
No hard localisation; locations recorded & risk-assessed.
Standard FSMA enforcement against the firm (not the provider).
Supervisory, principles-based; no judicial case law specific to SS2/21.
Reissued 18 Mar 2026 as Appendix 3 to PS7/26 with MTP examples.
PRA SS2/21, bankofengland.co.uk (PRA Rulebook & PS7/26, 18 Mar 2026).
Operational Resilience — PRA PS6/21 / SS1/21 & FCA PS21/3 (incl. SYSC 15A)
PRA PS6/21; SS1/21; FCA PS21/3
Mar 2021
31 Mar 2022; impact-tolerance compliance by 31 Mar 2025 (passed)
PRA & FCA
Banks, building societies, PRA investment firms, insurers, FCA enhanced-scope firms.
Important Business Services (IBS); set impact tolerances; remain within them through severe-but-plausible scenarios.
— (resilience standard; interacts with SS2/21).
Self-assessment & scenario testing; no separate hour-clock.
Self-assessment document maintained & available to supervisors.
Indirect.
Supervisory review of IBS mapping & tolerances.
Supervisory; no judicial case law.
Mapping & tolerance setting now BAU; feeds the PS7/26 incident regime.
PRA PS6/21 & SS1/21; FCA PS21/3, bankofengland.co.uk / fca.org.uk.
FCA SYSC 8 (Outsourcing) & FG16/5 (Cloud and third-party IT guidance)
SYSC 8; FG16/5
FG16/5 first issued 2016; updated
In force
FCA
FCA solo-regulated & dual-regulated firms.
Critical/important operational functions (SYSC 8.1).
Written agreement; rights of access & audit; data protection; business continuity; exit; sub-outsourcing oversight (FG16/5).
Notify FCA of material outsourcing (SUP 15); no hour-clock here.
Maintain oversight records; no formal submitted register.
Indirect.
No hard localisation; data-location risk managed.
FCA conduct enforcement against the firm.
Supervisory; no judicial case law specific to FG16/5.
Folded into the unified operational-incident & third-party regime (FCA PS26/2).
FCA Handbook SYSC 8; FCA FG16/5, fca.org.uk.
UK Critical Third Parties (CTP) Regime
FSMA 2000 Pt 9E ss.312L et seq. (inserted by FSMA 2023); PRA SS6/24 & SS7/24; FCA CTP Sourcebook; Joint PS PRA PS16/24 / FCA PS24/16 (12 Nov 2024)
Joint PS 12 Nov 2024
Regime in force 1 Jan 2025; obligations crystallise only on HMT designation
HM Treasury (designation) · PRA · FCA · BoE (oversight)
Third parties whose failure could threaten the stability of, or confidence in, the UK financial system. Entity-level designation.
'Systemic third party service' (STPS) is the regulated unit; 6 Fundamental Rules + 8 Operational Risk & Resilience Requirements (ORRRs).
Obligations run on the CTP directly, not via contract clauses (incident mgmt, scenario testing, supply-chain risk, governance, termination preparedness).
Self-assessment within 3 months of designation; mapping within 12 months; then annual self-assessment & scenario testing.
Self-assessment + mapping submitted to regulators.
DIRECT — regulators may bar a CTP from serving regulated firms or impose conditions.
Standard FSMA enforcement powers + skilled-person reviews (SS7/24).

STATUS (Jun 2026): NO designations made. Economic Secretary Lucy Rigby told Treasury Committee (4 Nov 2025) no firms confirmed as being assessed. HMT response to HC 684 (18 Mar 2026, HC 1791): expects 'to make initial designation decisions this year'. UK–EU MoU on CTP oversight signed Jan 2026.
Treasury Committee 'AI in Financial Services' (HC 684, 20 Jan 2026) urged designation of major AI/cloud providers by end-2026.
FSMA 2000 Pt 9E; PRA PS16/24 / FCA PS24/16; HMT response HC 1791 (18 Mar 2026), publications.parliament.uk.
PRA PS7/26 / FCA PS26/2 / BoE PS — Operational Incident & Third-Party Reporting (unified regime)
PRA PS7/26 (+ SS1/26); FCA PS26/2 (+ FG26/3, FG26/4); finalises PRA CP17/24 & FCA CP24/28 (Dec 2024)
Published 18 Mar 2026
Effective 18 Mar 2027 (12-month implementation; review +2 yrs)
PRA · FCA · BoE

Banks, building societies, PRA investment firms, insurers; FCA enhanced-scope firms; PSPs retain bespoke clock. Third-country branches excluded from notification; smaller credit unions removed by PRA.
Single cross-authority 'operational incident' definition; 'material third-party' (MTP) arrangement triggers reporting.
Pre-notification before entering/significantly changing an MTP arrangement; standardised templates.

INITIAL: 'as soon as practicable' & (FCA) within 24 HOURS of determining a reporting threshold is met ('reasonable belief'). FINAL report within 30 working days of resolution (extendable to 60). PSPs keep the 4-hour clock. Near-misses excluded.
Register of MTP arrangements; annual submission (one portal — FCA Connect).
Reporting regime; systemic-provider oversight remains in the CTP regime.
Late filing not penalised where firm acts promptly on reasonable belief and the incident later escalates.
New regime; no case law. Drivers cited: >40% of 2025 FCA-reported cyber incidents involved a third party; 2025 Cloudflare/AWS outages.
OPEN: BoE consulting on revoking Rule 4 of the Recognised Clearing House Rules Instrument 2018 (duplicative for CCPs) — closes 18 Jun 2026.
PRA PS7/26 & SS1/26; FCA PS26/2, FG26/3, FG26/4 (18 Mar 2026), bankofengland.co.uk / fca.org.uk.

EU.reg: European Commission · EBA · ESMA · EIOPA (ESAs) · NCAs
I: DORA — Digital Operational Resilience Act (Regulation)
Regulation (EU) 2022/2554
In force 16 Jan 2023
Applicable 17 Jan 2025
European Commission / ESAs / NCAs
≈20 categories of EU financial entities + ICT third-party service providers.
ICT services supporting 'critical or important functions' (CIF). Management body has ultimate responsibility (Art 5); personal liability up to €1m (national transposition).

Art 30 minimum clauses (mandatory in writing): function/service description & sub-outsourcing of CIF; locations + prior notice on relocation; data availability/integrity/confidentiality; access/recovery/return on insolvency or termination; SLAs; incident assistance; cooperation with competent authorities; termination rights; security-awareness participation. CIF adds: measurable targets; TLPT participation; unrestricted audit/inspection rights; exit strategies.
Major ICT-related incident: INITIAL within 4h of classification (no later than 24h from detection); INTERMEDIATE 72h; FINAL 1 month (RTS/ITS 2024/1772 + reporting ITS).
Register of Information (Art 28(3)) at entity/sub-consolidated/consolidated level; ANNUAL to NCA (by 30 Apr); first cycle 30 Apr 2025. ITS (EU) 2024/2956 (XBRL/XML).
DIRECT for CTPPs (Art 31–44).
Service/data locations specified; relocation needs prior notice. No hard localisation.
Financial entities: up to 2% of total annual worldwide turnover / €1m individuals (Art 50). CTPPs: 1% average daily worldwide turnover/day (Art 35(6)).
RoI data quality poor in first cycle: ESAs Dry Run (EBA, 17 Dec 2024) — only ~6.5% of registers passed all checks. Adoption still patchy 2026 (CSSF via EY Lux: ~40% submitted by 16 Mar 2026).
Q1 2026 RoI cycle first hard supervisory test (automated NCA cross-checks). Statutory auditors' review under Art 58 due by 17 Jan 2026. Annual CTPP re-designation expected.
Regulation (EU) 2022/2554; EBA Dry Run summary (17 Dec 2024), eur-lex.europa.eu / eba.europa.eu.
DORA RTS/ITS package (delegated & implementing acts)
(EU) 2024/1772 (incident classification); 2024/1773 (ICT 3P policy, CIF); 2024/1774 (risk-mgmt framework); 2024/2956 (RoI ITS); 2025/532 (subcontracting RTS); 2024/1502 (CTPP criticality criteria)
European Commission (on ESA drafts)
All DORA-scope entities & their ICT providers.
7 incident-classification criteria (2024/1772); CIF policy content (2024/1773).
2024/1773 prescribes ICT-third-party-policy content for CIF; 2025/532 governs subcontracting of CIF (pre-contract due diligence, chain monitoring, material-change notice).
Classification thresholds (2024/1772) feed the 4h/72h/1-month clock.
RoI template & taxonomy (2024/2956).
Criticality criteria for CTPP designation (2024/1502).
Per DORA (Art 50) / CTPP regime.
Draft Art 5 of the subcontracting RTS (subcontractor monitoring) REMOVED as exceeding the Art 30(5) mandate.
Further ESA guidelines & Q&As ongoing.
Commission Delegated/Implementing Regs as cited; EBA consultation; A&O Shearman / Bird & Bird, eur-lex.europa.eu.
DORA Oversight of Critical ICT Third-Party Providers (CTPP) — the named 19-provider list
DORA Art 31–44; designation under Art 31(9)
First designations 18 Nov 2025
Oversight live from designation
ESAs (Joint Oversight Committee) + assigned Lead Overseer
ICT providers designated as critical to the EU financial system.
Criticality per (EU) 2024/1502 (systemic impact, substitutability, concentration).
Oversight runs on the provider directly (recommendations, 'comply or explain'); non-EU CTPPs must establish an EU subsidiary within 12 months.
Via overseen entities' incident frameworks.
CTPP cooperation with Joint Examination Teams.

DIRECT. The 19 designated CTPPs (alphabetical, official ESA list): Accenture plc; Amazon Web Services EMEA Sàrl; Capgemini SE; Colt Technology Services; Deutsche Telekom AG; Equinix (EMEA) B.V.; Fidelity National Information Services (FIS); Google Cloud EMEA Ltd; IBM Corp.; InterXion HeadQuarters B.V.; Kyndryl Inc.; LSEG Data and Risk Ltd; Microsoft Ireland Operations Ltd; NTT DATA Inc.; Oracle Nederland B.V.; Orange SA; SAP SE; Tata Consultancy Services Ltd.
Non-EU CTPP must establish an EU subsidiary within 12 months.
Periodic penalty payments up to 1% of average daily worldwide turnover, up to 6 months (Art 35(6)); ESAs may, as a last resort, require entities to suspend/terminate use.
Designation itself is the first supervisory act; no enforcement decisions yet. CAVEAT: official ESA docs do NOT publish a provider-by-provider Lead Overseer assignment — do not assert one.
List updated at least annually; voluntary opt-in possible (Art 31(11)); 2026 Joint Examination Teams expected. Always cross-check the live ESA list.
ESAs joint press release (18 Nov 2025); DLA Piper / TLT / Katten, esma.europa.eu / eba.europa.eu / eiopa.europa.eu.

In force (NIS2 transposed nationally, with variation)

Financial entities & (NIS2) essential/important entities.

GDPR Art 28 processor clauses; EBA outsourcing-agreement content.
GDPR Art 33: 72h to supervisory authority. NIS2 Art 23: 24h early warning / 72h notification / 1-month final.
EBA §54 outsourcing register (LEI, country, sub-outsourcing, criticality, cloud model).
Indirect (sector-specific oversight via DORA).
Equivalence-based; no hard localisation.
GDPR up to €20m / 4% global turnover; NIS2 national fines.
Extensive GDPR enforcement (DPAs); NIS2 enforcement emerging.

CBUAE Outsourcing Regulation for Banks + Outsourcing Standards
Circular No. 14/2021 + Outsourcing Standards
31 May 2021 (Official Gazette No. 704)
15 Jul 2021; legacy-contract grace expired 31 Dec 2023
Central Bank of the UAE
All UAE banks, solo & group-wide; intra-group and external; within or outside the UAE.

'Material Business Activity' — disruption could significantly impact operations, reputation, financial stability or supervisory compliance (§1.9). Core banking & key control functions effectively cannot be outsourced.

§5 — 15 minimum clauses: scope; SLAs/KPIs; service & data-processing locations; sub-outsourcing controls & prior notice; audit rights for bank's internal/external auditors AND CBUAE on-site rights (§5.15); BCP/DR; data confidentiality; data return/destruction on termination; breach notice 'without undue delay' (§5.14); termination & exit assistance.
Breach/material event notified to CBUAE 'without undue delay' (§5.14); immediate notice of events materially affecting the bank.
Comprehensive register of all (material & non-material) arrangements, solo & group-wide; available to CBUAE on request (§9).
Indirect, but §8 requires prior CBUAE non-objection before outsourcing any material activity (incl. intra-group); CBUAE on-site rights over the provider (§5.15).
HARD: §6 Master System of Record maintained & stored within the UAE; confidential data not shared outside UAE without CBUAE approval + prior written customer consent.
Administrative fines, restrictions & licence actions under CBUAE Law (Decretal Federal Law 14/2018, as amended).
Supervisory/administrative; case-specific. No public judicial case law.
CBUAE cloud-computing standards require pre-engagement supervisory engagement for material cloud.
CBUAE Rulebook — Outsourcing Regulation & Standards, rulebook.centralbank.ae.

Records of outsourcing; provided to regulator on request.

Free-zone data-protection laws apply (DIFC DP Law 2020; ADGM DP Regs 2021).

IN.reg: RBI · SEBI · IRDAI · MeitY / Data Protection Board
I: RBI (NBFC / Commercial Banks — Managing Risks in Outsourcing) Directions, 2025
RBI/DOR/2025-26/363 (NBFC, s.45L RBI Act); RBI/DOR/2025-26/245 (Commercial Banks, s.35A BR Act). Withdrawals via DOR.RRC.REC.302/33-01-010/2025-26
Immediate; legacy IT-outsourcing agreements to comply by 10 Apr 2026 (or renewal, whichever earlier)
Reserve Bank of India

NBFCs (graded by layer — Base: general rules only; Middle+ : full IT provisions) & commercial banks. Foreign banks: 'comply or explain'. CONSOLIDATES & REPEALS the 2023 IT-outsourcing MD and financial-services outsourcing framework.

'Material outsourcing of IT services' — disruption/compromise would significantly impact operations or materially affect customers via unauthorised access/loss/theft of customer info. Internal audit, compliance, KYC approval & loan sanctioning CANNOT be outsourced.

Service standards; data confidentiality; audit rights; sub-outsourcing controls; UNRESTRICTED RBI inspection/access rights (data & premises) for RE, auditors & RBI; termination; BCP; full RE access to all data/systems.
IT service-provider security breach reported to RBI within 6 HOURS of detection by the service provider; banks immediately notify RBI of any breach/leak of confidential customer information.
Central register/inventory of material outsourcing; periodic dependency mapping; RBI inspection on demand.
Indirect; RBI direct audit/inspection rights over the provider (incl. those abroad).
HARD: outsourced data to be stored in India.
Monetary penalties under the Banking Regulation Act / RBI Act / PSS Act.
Precedent action: RBI barred Mastercard / Amex / Diners Club from onboarding new customers (Apr–Jul 2021) for payments-data-localisation breaches.
Replaces the draft 'Managing Risks & Code of Conduct in Outsourcing of Financial Services' (Oct 2023) — now subsumed.
RBI Master Directions, rbi.org.in (id=12941); Vinod Kothari Consultants; TaxGuru (28 Nov 2025).

20 Aug 2024 (supersedes all prior cyber circulars)

Securities and Exchange Board of India
All SEBI REs in 5 categories: MIIs, Qualified, Mid-size, Small, Self-certification.
Criticality of systems; MIIs & Qualified REs face the full regime.
Supply-chain/SBOM management; vendor security obligations; SOC (own/group/Market-SOC); audit rights.
Incident reporting via SEBI portal / authorised SRO; timelines per category.
Compliance reporting via SEBI portal; VAPT, SOC efficacy & audit reports.
Indirect.
Data generated in India to remain in India; if the data centre is abroad, a readable copy kept in India; cryptographic key management within India.
SEBI penalties up to ₹1 crore (or 3× profits) under the SEBI Act.
Supervisory; CERT-In-empanelled audits mandatory.
Technical clarifications continuing into 2025 (Aug 2025).

All payment-system operators & participants.

Indirect.

PSS Act penalties; onboarding bans.

DPDP Act 2023; DPDP Rules 2025 (Gazette G.S.R. 846(E))
Act 2023; Rules notified 13/14 Nov 2025

Data fiduciaries & processors handling digital personal data.
Significant Data Fiduciaries (SDFs) to be notified — extra obligations.
Security safeguards (encryption, access controls, ≥1-yr log retention) flowed down into processor contracts.
Breach notice to the Board and to affected data principals — within 72h of the Board notification.
Records of processing; safeguard documentation.
Cross-border transfer permitted subject to government conditions; certain categories may be localised (to be notified — Rule 13(4)).
Up to ₹250 crore for failure of security safeguards.

MAS Notice 658 (Banks) & 1121 (Merchant Banks) — Management of Outsourced Relevant Services
MAS Notice 658 / 1121 (Banking Act 1970)
Issued 11 Dec 2023
Effective 11 Dec 2024
Monetary Authority of Singapore
Banks & merchant banks. 'Outsourced Relevant Service' broad (Annex C: public cloud SaaS/PaaS/IaaS, data centres, IT helpdesk, HR); Annex B exclusions.
Material Ongoing Outsourced Relevant Services (MOORS) attract the full prescriptive regime. A lighter set applies to any service involving disclosure of customer information.

Written agreement with mandatory terms incl. audit rights for the bank, MAS & the bank's auditors; sub-contracting assessment + written prior notice 'within a reasonable period'; specified termination grounds (MAS direction; failure to safeguard customer-info; demonstrable deterioration; reasonable notice).
Per the TRM Notice; outsourcing-specific notifications to MAS.
Outsourcing Register (all MOORS + customer-info-disclosure services); submitted to MAS SEMI-ANNUALLY (≤15 business days after 30 Jun & 31 Dec) + on request.
Indirect; MAS audit/access rights over the provider.
Banking secrecy (Banking Act §47); customer-info safeguards required in MOORS contracts.
Banking Act penalties; supervisory capital add-ons.
More prescriptive than EBA/DORA ('must' vs proportionality).
MAS Guidelines on Outsourcing (Banks) & (FIs other than Banks) effective 11 Dec 2024, replacing the 2016 Guidelines.
MAS Notices 658/1121 + FAQ (11 Dec 2024), mas.gov.sg; PwC / Allen & Gledhill.

FSM-N05 (replaces cancelled Notice 644); Cyber Hygiene Notice
Effective 10 May 2024
Monetary Authority of Singapore

Final 6 Jun 2023; FR 9 Jun 2023
In effect (replaced OCC 2013-29, SR 13-19, FIL-44-2008)
OCC · Federal Reserve · FDIC (joint)
All banking organisations; all third-party business arrangements 'by contract or otherwise' incl. affiliates, fintech, JVs, payment processing, referral.
'Critical activities' guide the intensity of risk management (no rigid 'material' tier).
Principles-based lifecycle (planning, due diligence/selection, contract negotiation, ongoing monitoring, termination); bank retains responsibility.
No clock in this guidance (see Computer-Security Rule & SEC instruments).
No formal federal register; examination-readiness expected.
Indirect; service providers examinable under the Bank Service Company Act (12 USC 1867(c)).
No federal localisation.
Enforcement via the agencies' general powers.
Principles-based; supervisory.

NYDFS Cybersecurity Regulation (Part 500) + TPSP Industry Letter
23 NYCRR Part 500 (2nd Amendment); §500.11; NYDFS TPSP Industry Letter (21 Oct 2025)
2nd Amendment Nov 2023; Letter 21 Oct 2025
Final tranche (MFA for all individuals) effective 1 Nov 2025
New York Department of Financial Services
NYDFS-licensed 'Covered Entities'.
Risk-based; §500.11 governs TPSP risk.

§500.11 + Oct-2025 Letter recommend baseline clauses: access controls; encryption in transit/at rest; prompt cyber-event notice; compliance warranties; data-location/transfer restrictions; subcontractor rights; data-use/exit; AND clauses on acceptable AI use & whether the entity's data may train AI models.
§500.17(a): notify Superintendent ≤72h after determining a Cybersecurity Incident (incl. at a TPSP). §500.17(c): ransom payment ≤24h + written explanation within 30 days.
Internal TPSP inventory under §500.11 (risk tier, controls, MFA, encryption).
Indirect; NYDFS examines the Covered Entity's TPSP programme.
Data-location/transfer restrictions recommended in contracts.
NY Banking Law: $2,500/day; $15,000/day (reckless); $75,000/day (knowing/wilful).
NYDFS PayPal $2m (Jan 2025, Dec 2022 breach); NY AG/NYDFS GEICO+Travelers $11.3m total (25 Nov 2024, GEICO $9.75m / Travelers $1.55m); Genesis Global ($8m + BitLicense surrender).
Oct-2025 Letter sets the supervisory benchmark; NYDFS will weigh TPSP practices in exams/investigations/enforcement.
23 NYCRR Part 500; NYDFS Industry Letter (21 Oct 2025), dfs.ny.gov; NY AG press release (25 Nov 2024).

Adopted 15/16 May 2024

Sensitive customer information.

HKMA SPM SA-2 — Outsourcing (Supervisory Policy Manual)
SPM module SA-2; Banking Ordinance 7th Schedule paras 10 & 12
Issued 28 Dec 2001 (supersedes 1996 Guideline 3.8 & 1998 circular)
Current
Hong Kong Monetary Authority
Authorised Institutions (AIs).
Risk-based & proportionate; outsourcing must not impair internal controls.

§5 contractual provisions: scope; service levels; confidentiality; BCP; audit rights; sub-outsourcing notice/approval; governing law (preferably HK); dispute resolution; termination. §8 — HKMA access/inspection of the provider.
TM-E-1 / OR-2 expect reporting 'as soon as practicable'; typically within hours.
Pre-engagement notification of material outsourcing to HKMA (case-by-case).
Indirect; HKMA inspection rights over the provider (§8).
Customer-data protection (§6); PDPO §33 cross-border transfer.

Supervisory; no judicial case law.

NFRA Measures for Data Security Management of Banking and Insurance Institutions
NFRA Regulation [2024] No. 24 (银行保险机构数据安全管理办法)
Issued & effective 27 Dec 2024 (immediate)
National Financial Regulatory Administration

PRC commercial/policy banks, rural co-ops, AMCs, financial leasing, auto/consumer finance, money brokers, trust & wealth-management cos, insurers & insurance asset managers, financial holding cos; 'other banking FIs' read to include branches of foreign banks.
Data classified (Art 11 et seq., per GB/T 43697-2024): Core / Important / General (+ 'sensitive' sub-class). Core functions cannot be outsourced.

Contracts must specify processing scope, responsibilities & exit protocols; 'business necessity' & 'least privilege' (Art 53); third-party/outsourcing risk monitoring (Art 65); data destruction on termination incl. on-site verification (Art 38).

Art 69: report data-security incident to NFRA/local office within 2 HOURS of occurrence; written report within 24h; progress updates every 2h for 'particularly major' incidents (+ user notice + public-security report); comprehensive evaluation report within 5 business days of resolution.
Annual data-security risk-assessment report to NFRA by 15 Jan; ≥20-business-day pre-filing before certain data-transfer/sharing contracts.
Indirect; NFRA approval required to share important/higher-level data with other FIs.
Effective localisation via DSL/PIPL/CII; cross-border subject to CAC mechanisms.
Art 77: fines up to CNY 500,000 (banks) / CNY 300,000 (insurers); up to suspension & licence revocation.
Enforcement ranges from warnings to suspension/licence revocation; emerging.
NFRA to issue an important-data catalogue (Art 71); dual-oversight tension with PBOC classifications.
NFRA Reg [2024] No. 24, nfra.gov.cn; Han Kun / Linklaters; GB/T 43697-2024.

APRA Prudential Standard CPS 230 — Operational Risk Management
CPS 230
Final 17 Jul 2023
Effective 1 Jul 2025; pre-existing contracts comply by earlier of next renewal or 1 Jul 2026
Australian Prudential Regulation Authority
Cross-industry — ADIs, insurers, RSE (super) licensees. Replaces CPS/SPS 231 (Outsourcing) & CPS/SPS 232 (BCM).

Critical operations (deemed: ADIs — payments, deposit-taking, customer functions; insurers — claims; RSE — fund admin, custody, investment mgmt). 'Material service providers' (incl. underwriting, claims, brokerage, reinsurance for insurers).
Paras 52–55: comprehensive policy; due diligence (para 53); legally binding agreement (para 54); performance monitoring; fourth-party oversight (para 48(c)). Paras 56–58: orderly exit.

Para 41: notify APRA as soon as possible & ≤72h after becoming aware of an operational-risk incident likely to have material financial impact / material impact on critical operations. BCP activation notified within 24h. Material service-provider changes within 20 business days.
Para 51: MSP Register — first submission requested 1 Oct 2025; annual + event-based via APRA Connect.
Indirect; APRA may review the provider via the regulated entity.
Prudential enforcement (directions, licence conditions, capital).
Supervisory programme: 2025–26 review of a subset of entities; 2026–27 expanded; 2027–28 BAU.
See NTSP amendments.
APRA CPS 230 & CPG 230, apra.gov.au.

LIMITED EXEMPTION from specified obligations (paras 54, 55, 56(d), 57, 58(a), 58(c)) where contractual compliance is not practicable and bespoke terms cannot be negotiated. All other CPS 230 obligations remain.
Unchanged (CPS 230 para 41).
MSP Register template updated to flag exempt arrangements; updated APRA Connect return.
Indirect.
As CPS 230.
APRA may add providers to the Attachment at its discretion (changes which providers qualify).
